bibliographic.citation: The Stonor Letters and Papers, 1290–1483, ed. Charles Lethbridge Kingsford, 2 vols., Camden Third Series 29–30 (London: Offices of the Royal Historical Society, 1919).
bibliographic.publisher: Offices of the Royal Historical Society
bibliographic.title: The Stonor Letters and Papers, 1290–1483

entities.description: The immediate two-volume source edition. Kingsford selected and arranged 333 numbered units from a dispersed documentary archive, printed almost all identified letters, gave full copies or abstracts of selected deeds and other records, added a few illustrative documents outside the historical Stonor archive, and supplied source notes, editorial dates, introduction, calendars, glossary, and indexes.
entities.label: Kingsford's 1919 Stonor Letters and Papers

rights.basis: The underlying records are medieval and the edition was published in 1919, more than 95 years ago; U-M independently labels the corresponding item public domain in the United States.
rights.component: The medieval documentary texts, Kingsford's 1919 edited text, and 1919 editorial matter
rights.jurisdiction: United States

next_steps: Create an item-level table from each Kingsford headnote and appendix entry to current Discovery records.
Record whether each source is printed in full, excerpted, abstracted, merely cited, grouped with another document, or added from outside the historical Stonor archive.
Confirm current references directly with TNA where catalog rearrangement prevents a mechanical crosswalk.
openQuestions.question: What is the exact current TNA reference and representation type for every source document in Kingsford's 333 numbered units and appendices?

next_steps: Consult historical Early Chancery Proceedings finding aids and TNA transfer/renumbering documentation.
Ask TNA whether 31/34–36 was a legacy range, a typesetting error, or a designation for multiple membranes now cataloged under C 1/31/534.
openQuestions.question: Why does Kingsford cite Early Chancery Proceedings 31/34–36 for John Frende v. Richard Fortescue while the exact current TNA record is C 1/31/534?

next_steps: Compare Carpenter's supplementary listing item by item with the current Discovery export.
Distinguish physical documents, fragments, catalog items, and separately described recto/verso components.
Keep C 47/37/23 separate because it was added in 1998 and was unavailable to Carpenter's 1996 edition.
openQuestions.question: How do Carpenter's and Noble's 152 later-found documents or fragments correspond to the current C 47/37/22 hierarchy, whose numbered items reach at least 157 and include verso component records?

next_steps: Obtain written clarification from U-M and PIMS/Internet Archive before redistributing their page-image files.
Rely on separately generated text or a clearly licensed source for publication workflows unless permission is confirmed.
Keep the public-domain status of the 1919 content separate from provider terms for modern digital files.
openQuestions.question: What reuse terms govern the exact U-M raw TIFF package and the independent PIMS/Internet Archive scan derivatives?

next_steps: Consult Truelove's 2001 thesis and subsequent publication history.
Ask the author or repository about access to complete revised transcriptions.
Treat Kingsford's readings and U-M's uncorrected OCR as fallible when researching manuscript letterforms, abbreviations, or exact wording.
openQuestions.question: Is a complete scholarly edition or accessible corpus of Alison Truelove's revised Stonor transcriptions available for systematic comparison?

reviewNotes: The cataloged work is Kingsford's editorially assembled selection, not a single authored medieval work and not the complete historical Stonor archive.
The XML header's DA 20 .R9 Ser.3 v.29-30 is a library call number for the print series, not an archival shelfmark.
Historical Public Record Office designations and modern TNA shelfmarks are preserved separately; current catalog dates do not replace Kingsford's conjectural document dates.
Later-recovered C 47/37/22 and C 47/37/23 material is relevant for comparison but is not placed upstream of 1919, 1924, or CME without evidence of use.

lineage.summary: The repository XML is a U-M-derived version of CME item ACA1723.0001.001, created in 2006 by uncorrected OCR from 460 linked page images of Charles Lethbridge Kingsford's 1919 two-volume edition. Kingsford reconstructed an editorial selection from dispersed Public Record Office records, chiefly Ancient Correspondence now in TNA SC 1, with selected Chancery Miscellanea now in C 47, Ancient Deeds now in C 146, a Ministers' Account now in SC 6, and an Early Chancery Proceedings item now cataloged as C 1/31/534. The electronic text retains the 333 numbered documents and Kingsford's headnotes but omits most print introduction, appendices, glossary, and indexes. A 1924 supplement, Carpenter's 1996 reissue, and later-recovered C 47/37/22 and /23 material are recorded as later layers, not sources of the 1919 edition or CME encoding.

catalogSummary.author: Various named and anonymous documentary creators
catalogSummary.date: Documents dated or assigned c. 1290–11 October 1483; edited selection first published 1919
catalogSummary.region: England, especially Stonor and the family's Oxfordshire and Thames Valley networks
catalogSummary.title: The Stonor Letters and Papers, 1290–1483

catalogingSubject.label: The Stonor Letters and Papers, 1290–1483
catalogingSubject.scope_note: Charles Lethbridge Kingsford's two-volume 1919 editorial reconstruction and selection of Stonor-family documents, represented here by 333 numbered editorial units. The cataloging subject is not one medieval authored work, not the whole historical Stonor archive, not every surviving Stonor document, and not 333 necessarily independent works: individual units may print one or more letters, deeds, accounts, fragments, abstracts, or illustrative records. Kingsford's edition, its archival witnesses, U-M's page-image/OCR encoding, later supplements and reprints, and the repository XML remain distinct lineage layers.

contentParts.label: Volume I: items 1–158
contentParts.summary: The first 158 numbered documentary units, from the charter of Richard de Stonor assigned c. 1290 through material dated or assigned to 1475.

contentParts.label: Volume II: items 159–333
contentParts.summary: The final 175 numbered documentary units, continuing the collection through Francis Lovell's letter to Sir William Stonor of 11 October 1483.

notes: Many headings supply approximate or conjectural dates. The lower endpoint belongs to Kingsford's title and his dating of item 1; the current TNA catalog gives that deed the broader range 1200–1300.

scope.description: Dates of creation, issue, or editorial assignment for the selected historical documents
First publication of Kingsford's two-volume edited selection

extent.description: The repository XML contains 333 body DIV1 item units, numbered 1–333, and 55 nested DIV2 sections. These are edition/encoding units, not a count of distinct archival objects: some units combine multiple documents, fragments, abstracts, or editorial material. Volume I contains 158 numbered units and volume II contains 175.

formStatements.description: A prose documentary collection comprising letters, charters, legal and administrative records, accounts, inventories, petitions, memoranda, and editorial abstracts.

genres.label: Correspondence
notes: Kingsford counted 256 letters excluding fragments; neither that count nor the title makes every numbered unit a letter.

languageStatements.label: English
notes: The U-M header declares only eng; direct inspection shows that this is not a complete description of the historical documents' languages.

scope.description: Kingsford's titles, abstracts, headnotes, introduction, notes, and reference matter

notes: The header value DA 20 .R9 Ser.3 v.29-30 is a bibliographic/library call number for the Camden volumes, not a manuscript or archival shelfmark.
The 333 numbered units are Kingsford's editorial sequence; the historical archive was larger, dispersed, and subsequently augmented by recovered material.
Document dates and titles often encode Kingsford's editorial judgment and should not be substituted for current repository descriptions without attribution.

next_steps: Create an item-level crosswalk recording each heading, documentary type, creator, recipient, language, date status, archival source, and whether Kingsford printed a full text or abstract.
Preserve multiple source documents within one numbered unit rather than forcing a one-to-one model.
openQuestions.question: Can all 333 numbered editorial units be cataloged at component level without treating grouped documents, abstracts, and fragments as equivalent units?

next_steps: Review the deed C 146/221 diplomatically and consult current scholarship on Richard de Stonor and Cicely.
Continue to attribute c. 1290 to Kingsford rather than silently replacing or hardening it.
openQuestions.question: Should the collection's lower date remain Kingsford's c. 1290 or be displayed with the current TNA catalog's broader 1200–1300 range for item 1?

next_steps: Perform item-level language tagging against the scans and source records.
Do not use the U-M header's eng-only declaration as a document-level language inventory.
openQuestions.question: What are the exact document-level language counts, including mixed-language and editorially abstracted units?

notes: Individual documents also concern or were written in continental locations, including Valladolid, Calais, and Bruges; England is not asserted as the place of every item's creation.

scope.description: Document headnotes, editorial dating, introduction, notes, appendices, glossary, and indexes in the print edition

subjects.label: Wars of the Roses and political news

summaries.value: Kingsford's edited collection reconstructs a dispersed archive associated with the Stonor family, established Oxfordshire gentry whose legal, estate, household, mercantile, familial, and political networks extended across England and beyond. The selected records range from a thirteenth-century charter to Francis Lovell's letter of 11 October 1483 and include family and business correspondence, deeds, petitions, writs, accounts, rentals, inventories, and other records. The collection is especially rich for fifteenth-century gentry life, literacy and letter writing, estate and household management, legal practice, commerce and the wool trade, and political experience during the Wars of the Roses.

The 1919 edition has 333 numbered units: volume I contains items 1–158 and volume II contains items 159–333. Kingsford printed almost all identified letters except fragments he judged too slight, supplied full texts or abstracts of selected deeds, included selected Chancery Miscellanea material, and added a few illustrative records that he expressly said did not belong to the historical archive. His print volumes also contain an introduction, appendices and calendars, a glossary, and indexes; the U-M electronic text omits most of that front and back matter while retaining linked page milestones.

This record describes Kingsford's 1919 editorially created selection as the browsable corpus work. Component-document dates, languages, genres, authorship, and archive references are scoped to selected passages rather than propagated to every unit. Later recovered material, the 1924 supplement, Carpenter's 1996 reissue, modern scans, U-M OCR/markup, and current TNA catalog descriptions are documented in lineage rather than conflated with the selected work.

work.author: Various named and anonymous documentary creators
work.dateDisplay: Documents dated or assigned c. 1290–11 October 1483; edited selection first published 1919

publication.embeddedTitle: The Stonor letters and papers, 1290-1483; ed. for the Royal historical society, from the origial documents in the Public record office, by Charles Lethbridge Kingsford.

work.regionDisplay: England, especially Stonor and the family's Oxfordshire and Thames Valley networks

subjects.label: Wars of the Roses and political news

The selected records range from a thirteenth-century charter to Francis Lovell's letter of 11 October 1483 and include family and business correspondence, deeds, petitions, writs, accounts, rentals, inventories, and other records. The collection is especially rich for fifteenth-century gentry life, literacy and letter writing, estate and household management, legal practice, commerce and the wool trade, and political experience during the Wars of the Roses.
